findings.title: System account 'postgres' has interactive login shell
findings.description: The system account 'postgres' (UID 113) is configured with an active login shell (/bin/bash). System accounts should be locked and prevented from logging in directly.
findings.remediation: Change the user shell to a non-interactive shell: usermod -s /usr/sbin/nologin postgres

findings.title: Process 'xfce4-session' running from deleted binary
findings.description: The process 'xfce4-session' (PID 1174) is executing from a binary that has been deleted from the filesystem (/usr/bin/xfce4-session (deleted)). While common during system upgrades before services are restarted, this is also a classic technique used by attackers to hide malware.
findings.remediation: Restart the affected service: systemctl restart xfce4-session (or kill PID 1174 if unauthorized).

findings.title: Scanner run without root privileges
findings.description: The scanner is executed as a non-root user. Many system audits, configuration reviews, and security checks will fail or return incomplete results due to permission restrictions.
findings.remediation: Run the vulnerability scanner with root privileges: sudo python3 nullhound.py

findings.title: Insecure kernel setting: kernel.dmesg_restrict
findings.description: Unprivileged users can view kernel syslog messages (dmesg). This can leak kernel addresses or memory contents useful for local kernel exploits.
findings.remediation: Add or update the setting in /etc/sysctl.conf: kernel.dmesg_restrict = 1, then load it: sudo sysctl -p

findings.title: Insecure kernel setting: kernel.kptr_restrict
findings.description: Kernel pointer addresses are exposed to unprivileged users, defeating kernel ASLR.
findings.remediation: Add or update the setting in /etc/sysctl.conf: kernel.kptr_restrict = 2, then load it: sudo sysctl -p

findings.title: Insecure kernel setting: kernel.yama.ptrace_scope
findings.description: Yama ptrace scope is set to unrestricted, allowing process memory reading/injection between users, enabling process hijacking.
findings.remediation: Add or update the setting in /etc/sysctl.conf: kernel.yama.ptrace_scope = 1, then load it: sudo sysctl -p

findings.title: Insecure kernel setting: net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter
findings.description: Reverse Path Filtering is disabled, making the system vulnerable to IP address spoofing attacks.
findings.remediation: Add or update the setting in /etc/sysctl.conf: net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter = 1, then load it: sudo sysctl -p

findings.title: Insecure kernel setting: net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects
findings.description: System accepts ICMP redirect messages, allowing attackers to route traffic via malicious routes.
findings.remediation: Add or update the setting in /etc/sysctl.conf: net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0, then load it: sudo sysctl -p

findings.title: Insecure kernel setting: net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects
findings.description: IPv6 system accepts ICMP redirect messages, allowing routing redirection attacks.
findings.remediation: Add or update the setting in /etc/sysctl.conf: net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0, then load it: sudo sysctl -p

findings.title: Insecure kernel setting: net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects
findings.description: System sends ICMP redirect messages. Hosts should only send redirects if they act as gateway routers.
findings.remediation: Add or update the setting in /etc/sysctl.conf: net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0, then load it: sudo sysctl -p

findings.title: Insecure SSH config: x11forwarding set to 'yes'
findings.description: X11 forwarding is enabled. Malicious local users can hijack remote graphical sessions.
findings.remediation: Edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config, modify the line to 'x11forwarding no', and reload sshd: sudo systemctl reload sshd

findings.title: SSH idle timeout is not configured
findings.description: ClientAliveInterval is disabled or not set. Inactive SSH sessions will remain open indefinitely, increasing the risk of unauthorized session hijacking on unattended terminals.
findings.remediation: Configure 'ClientAliveInterval 300' and 'ClientAliveCountMax 3' in sshd_config.

findings.title: No active firewall detected
findings.description: The system does not have an active firewall configuration (UFW, Firewalld, iptables, or nftables). An unprotected system exposes all running network services directly to the network, increasing the risk of unauthorized access.
findings.evidence: No active firewall rules or processes running.
findings.remediation: Install and enable a firewall management utility: 'sudo apt install ufw && sudo ufw enable' or 'sudo dnf install firewalld && sudo systemctl enable --now firewalld'.

findings.title: /tmp partition is not mounted with 'noexec'
findings.description: The /tmp partition is not configured with 'noexec' in mount options. Staging and executing malware directly from temporary directories is a common attacker tactic.
findings.remediation: Edit /etc/fstab and add 'noexec,nosuid,nodev' to the /tmp mount options, then remount.

findings.title: Unusual SUID/SGID binary detected
findings.description: An SUID or SGID binary was found that is not in the baseline of common/standard system binaries. SUID files run with owner permissions (often root) and are key targets for privilege escalation.
findings.remediation: Verify if the SUID bit is necessary for these binaries. If not, remove it: sudo chmod u-s <file>

findings.title: Hidden directory in sensitive path
findings.description: A hidden directory was found directly in a sensitive root directory. Attackers often prefix backdoor folders with a '.' to hide them from simple listings.
findings.remediation: Inspect the directory contents to ensure they are legitimate configurations or assets.

findings.title: Cron script referenced in 'apt-compat' is world-writable
findings.description: The script '/dev/urandom' executed by a cron job in '/etc/cron.daily/apt-compat' is world-writable. A local attacker can edit the script contents to execute code with the privileges of the cron job (usually root).
findings.remediation: Restrict write permission to owner: chmod o-w /dev/urandom
